title: Apply: First Amendment: Speech and Press
description: Apply your understanding of the modern cases and debates surrounding the First Amendment's Speech and Press Clauses with this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: First Amendment: Speech and Press—Modern Cases and Debates
Apply: First Amendment: Speech and Press

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Representative John Nicholas’s comments in a debate about the Sedition Act in the House of Representatives in 1798.**

Under such circumstances it must be seen that the printers of papers would be [[☃ definition 1]] from printing anything which should be in the least offensive to a power which might so greatly harass them. They would not only [[☃ definition 2]] from publishing anything of the least questionable nature, but they would be afraid of publishing the truth, as, though true, it might not always be in their power to establish the truth to the satisfaction of a court of justice. This bill would, therefore, go to the suppression of every printing press in the country which is not [[☃ definition 3]] to the will of Government.

**Which statement best describes Nicholas’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Sedition Act punishes only those who seek to overthrow the government, making it constitutional.
The Sedition Act is an example of a constitutional limit on freedom of speech because it bans only inaccurate information.
The Sedition Act is wrong because it will suppress so much speech, including factually correct speech.
The Sedition Act is an example of federal overreach, as only the states can limit freedom of speech.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Representative John Nicholas’s comments in a debate about the Sedition Act in the House of Representatives in 1798.**

Under such circumstances it must be seen that the printers of papers would be [[☃ definition 1]] from printing anything which should be in the least offensive to a power which might so greatly harass them. They would not only [[☃ definition 2]] from publishing anything of the least questionable nature, but they would be afraid of publishing the truth, as, though true, it might not always be in their power to establish the truth to the satisfaction of a court of justice. This bill would, therefore, go to the suppression of every printing press in the country which is not [[☃ definition 3]] to the will of Government.

**The ideas expressed in the excerpt are also directly addressed in which Supreme Court case?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *Schenck v. United States* (1919)
*Abrams v. United States* (1919)
*West Virginia Board of Education v. Barnette* (1943)
*New York Times v. Sullivan* (1964)

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Court’s opinion in *Abrams v. United States* (1919).**

[T]he language of these [[☃ definition 1]] was obviously intended to provoke and to encourage resistance to the United States in the war, as the third count runs, and the defendants, in terms, plainly urged and advocated a [[☃ definition 2]] to a general strike of workers in ammunition factories for the purpose of [[☃ definition 3]] the production of [[☃ definition 4]] necessary and essential to the [[☃ definition 5]] of the war as is charged in the fourth count. Thus, it is clear not only that some evidence, but that much persuasive evidence, was before the jury tending to prove that the defendants were guilty as charged in both the third and fourth counts of the indictment, and, under the long established rule of law hereinbefore stated, the judgment of the district court must be Affirmed.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: doing something to achieve a goal, because there seem to be no other options
the act of carrying out some plan of action

choices.content: The government cannot place any limitations on speech, even if that speech criticizes their actions in a war.
The government can suppress anti-war speech, as such speech would threaten the war effort and endanger the public.
The government must not discourage opposing views, as it’s the government’s responsibility to promote freedom of speech.
The government can suppress speech if the speech spreads inaccurate information about the government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Justice Oliver Wendell Holmes’ dissenting opinion in *Abrams v. United States* (1919).**

But, as against dangers peculiar to war, as against others, the principle of the right to free speech is always the same. It is only the present danger of immediate evil or an intent to bring it about that [[☃ definition 1]] Congress in setting a limit to the expression of opinion where private rights are not concerned. Congress certainly cannot forbid all effort to change the mind of the country.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The right to free speech cannot be suppressed  if the speech creates a clear and present danger to Congress.
The right to free speech is essential, and Congress cannot limit someone’s right to try to influence public opinion.
The right to free speech prevents the government from issuing warrants to arrest those who discuss crimes but don’t carry them out.
The right to free speech is unlimited, and Congress cannot ban any speech, regardless of the content.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Court’s opinion in *West Virginia State Board of Education v. Barnette* (1943).**

We think the action of the local authorities in [[☃ definition 2]] [public school students to engage in] the flag salute and pledge [[☃ definition 1]] constitutional limitations on their power and invades the sphere of intellect and spirit which it is the purpose of the First Amendment to our Constitution to [[☃ definition 3]] from all official control.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Forcing public school students to participate in the flag salute and the pledge violates the First Amendment.
Encouraging people to salute and pledge to the flag is within the government’s powers.
Requiring people to salute and pledge to the flag does not violate people’s religious freedoms.
Compelling people to participate in the flag salute and the pledge is not a power expressly given to the federal government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Justice Hugo Black’s concurring opinion in the *Pentagon Papers* case.**

In the First Amendment the Founding Fathers gave the free press the protection it must have to fulfill its essential role in our democracy. The press was to serve the governed, not the governors. The Government's power to censor the press was abolished so that the press would remain forever free to [[☃ definition 1]] the Government. The press was protected so that it could bare the secrets of government and inform the people. Only a free and unrestrained press can effectively expose deception in government. 

**According to the excerpt, what is the purpose of the freedom of the press?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To keep the public informed about everyday government actions
To stop ordinary citizens from silencing unfavorable publications
To publish the viewpoints of both the majority and the minority
To question the government’s actions and decisions

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from James Madison’s “Report on the Virginia Resolutions,” published in 1800.***

This security of the freedom of the press requires that it should be [[☃ definition 1]] not only from previous restraint by the Executive, as in Great Britain, but from legislative restraint also; and this exemption, to be [[☃ definition 2]], must be an exemption not only from the previous inspection of [[☃ definition 3]], but from the [[☃ definition 4]] penalty of laws.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To ensure that the press can act as a check on the government, only laws passed by Congress can censor or restrict the publication of articles.
Freedom of the press can be restricted before and after publication to protect the government from people planning dangerous rebellions.
Freedom of the press can be restricted before and after publication to protect the government from those seeking revolution.
To ensure freedom of the press, the government must be responsible for securing the safety of reporters by protecting them from public violence.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Henry Lee’s “Report of the Minority on the Virginia Resolutions,” published in 1799.**

[T]he liberty of the press is a term which has a definite and appropriate [[☃ definition 1]], completely understood. It signifies a liberty to publish, free from previous restraint, any thing and every thing at the [[☃ definition 2]] of the printer only, but not the liberty of spreading with [[☃ definition 3]] false and scandalous [[☃ definition 4]] which may destroy the peace and mangle the reputation of an individual or of a community.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: freedom to make one’s own choices

choices.content: Freedom of the press means that people can publish what they like, but they must accept any consequences of publishing untrue and harmful statements.
Freedom of the press means that printers have absolute power to publish anything they wish without any form of accountability.
Freedom of the press requires that journalists ensure that any information they publish does not cause public distress.
Freedom of the press can be restricted by the government if the publication reveals truths that can harm the public’s perception of the government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Henry Lee’s “Report of the Minority on the Virginia Resolutions,” published in 1799.**

[T]he liberty of the press is a term which has a definite and appropriate [[☃ definition 1]], completely understood. It signifies a liberty to publish, free from previous restraint, any thing and every thing at the [[☃ definition 2]] of the printer only, but not the liberty of spreading with [[☃ definition 3]] false and scandalous [[☃ definition 4]] which may destroy the peace and mangle the reputation of an individual or of a community.

**Which document most closely aligns with the argument presented in this excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: freedom to make one’s own choices

choices.content: The First Amendment to the United States Constitution, which guarantees freedom of speech and the press without exception
The Virginia Statute for Religious Freedom, which emphasized the importance of unrestricted freedom of conscience and expression
The Sedition Act of 1798, which made it illegal to publish slanderous statements about the government
The Articles of Confederation, which did not grant the federal government any power to regulate the press

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from James Wilson’s comments at the Pennsylvania Ratifying Convention in 1787.**

The idea of the liberty of the press, is not carried so far as this permitting [[☃ definition 1]] to go unpunished in any country—what is meant by the liberty of the press is, that there should be no [[☃ definition 2]] restraint upon it; but that every author is responsible when he attacks the security or welfare of the government, or the safety, character and property of the individual.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: published statements that are false and damaging to someone’s reputation

choices.content: A free press can be restricted both before and after publication, if the information published is deemed a threat to national security.
Any statements made by journalists must be proven to be absolutely true; otherwise it’s the government’s responsibility to punish those journalists.
A free press needs to be able to publish journalists’ thoughts and feelings, even when untrue, without fear of punishment.
Although the government should not restrict the press before publication, journalists can be punished for their statements after publication.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from *Commentaries on the Laws of England* (1765) by William Blackstone. The work was extremely influential on the founders and American lawyers during and after the American Revolution. **

The liberty of the press is indeed essential to the nature of a free state: but this consists in laying no previous restraints upon publications, and not in freedom from [[☃ definition 1]] for criminal matter when published. Every freeman has an undoubted right to lay what [[☃ definition 2]] he pleases before the public: to forbid this is to destroy the freedom of the press: but if he publishes what is improper, [[☃ definition 3]], or illegal, he must take the consequences of his own [[☃ definition 4]].

**Which statement best summarizes Blackstone’s argument about freedom of the press?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: People should have the right to publish what they want, but the press can be punished if the speech is harmful or illegal.
Freedom of the press guarantees that all published material is protected from any form of legal consequence, regardless of its content.
Any restrictions on freedom of the press must be made before publication, as it is up to the government to monitor the press.
To legally restrict the press, the government must prove that the speech itself violates its laws.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a 1774 letter from the Continental Congress to the Inhabitants of the Province of Quebec.**

The last right we shall mention regards the freedom of the press. The importance of this consists, besides the advancement of truth, science, morality, and arts in general, in its [[☃ definition 1]] of [[☃ definition 2]] [[☃ definition 3]] on the administration of Government, its ready communication of thoughts between subjects, and its [[☃ definition 4]] promotion of union among them, whereby oppressive officers are shamed or intimidated into more honorable and just modes of conducting affairs.

**According to the excerpt, what is the purpose of the freedom of the press?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To help people come to consensus about the specific political beliefs that all citizens must adopt
To facilitate open discourse and hold those in power accountable
To serve as an official mouthpiece for the government, exclusively relaying its official pronouncements
To provide detailed instructions on how to become a scientist or artist
